title: Ziyu Shao, 16, Just Won the 50-Year-Old Bachauer Piano Competition in Salt Lake City
subtitle: Five teenage pianists took the stage with the Utah Symphony for the finals of the Gina Bachauer International — a competition that had 316 applications from 28 countries.
excerpt: The five finalists were all 16 or 17 years old. They played Chopin, Liszt, Rachmaninoff, and Tchaikovsky with a full symphony behind them. And they're calling themselves "the prodigies of tomorrow."
body:
Abravanel Hall in downtown Salt Lake City has a very good stage for a piano concert. Its walls are lined with gold leaf, its ceiling holds a chandelier the size of a small car, and its seven-foot Steinway sits under stage lights bright enough to read a phonebook by. On the evening of Saturday, June 27, that stage belonged to five pianists — every one of them either 16 or 17 years old — playing the final round of the 2026 Gina Bachauer International Piano Competition alongside the full Utah Symphony orchestra.

The Bachauer is 50 years old this year. It's named for Gina Bachauer, a legendary Greek pianist of the mid-20th century, and it was founded in Salt Lake City by her student Paul Pollei. This year the competition drew 316 applications from 28 countries. Judges narrowed those down to 160 who played 30-minute concerts in five different cities — Hamburg, New York, Seoul, Shanghai, and Salt Lake City. Then 56 traveled to Utah for the quarterfinals. Then five made the concerto final.

This year, for both competitions we had 316 applications from 28 countries. Out of all of those we invited 160 to perform 30-minute concerts in five different international cities.
— Kary Billings, Executive Director, Gina Bachauer International Piano Foundation

The final five, and what they played

A concerto is a piece written for a solo instrument (in this case, piano) plus a full orchestra. The finalists had a choice of famously difficult concertos to perform — pieces by Chopin, Liszt, Rachmaninoff, and Tchaikovsky — with the Utah Symphony reading along and reacting to every tempo change the young pianist made. Playing a concerto with a live orchestra is one of the hardest tests in classical music: you're not just playing your part, you're leading around 60 professional musicians who have to follow you.

When the jury announced its decision, Ziyu Shao, 16, of China took first place. Eunha Basu, 16, of the United States took second, and Jaeong Lee, 17, of South Korea took third. Two more finalists — Yichen Sang, 17, of China and Matthew Chang, 17, of the United States — earned finalist prizes. The Bachauer Foundation awarded more than $50,000 in prize money across the field.

Where the pianists actually practiced

A tournament like this brings up the question every touring pianist faces: where do you find a good piano to practice on when you land in a new city? Bachauer's answer is unusually charming. The foundation matches every visiting competitor with a local Salt Lake City host home — a family who has a piano and is willing to let a total stranger practice on it for two weeks.

The spirit of hospitality and giving is so strong here in Utah that we found 56 homes with pianos for all these competitors for nearly two weeks. Here, the competitors feel like they're part of the family. They just feel something very close and personal when they come to Utah.
— Kary Billings, Bachauer Foundation

Two of the five finalists rehearsed on a 1912 Steinway inside the First United Methodist Church in downtown Salt Lake City. "It's been fun to listen to," said Mike Green, the church's trustee.

"The prodigies of tomorrow"

You would probably expect a teenage classical piano competition to be intense and stressful. And by the practice hours involved, it is — competitors keep up several hours a day at the keyboard even while traveling. But everyone the Daily Utah Chronicle spoke to came back to the same word: joy.

These are the prodigies of tomorrow. To have the opportunity to just enjoy great classical music from such young artists, it's just an honor and a privilege to be able to experience them firsthand and get to know them on a personal basis and make new friends across the world.
— Mike Green, trustee, First United Methodist Church of Salt Lake City

The Bachauer Foundation says it is already planning the next 50 years — starting with a new competition cycle in 2027.
kidTake: In Salt Lake City, Utah, five kids around 16 or 17 years old played the piano in front of a whole big orchestra — the Utah Symphony — in a very famous concert hall. It was the final of a competition that started with 316 kids from 28 different countries. A 16-year-old girl named Ziyu Shao, from China, won first place. All five kids stayed with families in Salt Lake City who let them practice on their pianos every day for two weeks!
familyDiscussion: If you could learn any instrument, which one would you pick — and why that one?
The finalists all had to travel far from home and stay with families they had never met. What would be the scariest part of that? What would be the best part?
It takes about 10,000 hours of practice to become really great at something. What's something you already practice — and how many hours do you think you've put in?

glossary.meaning: A piece of music written for one solo instrument (like piano or violin) plus a full orchestra. Usually it's in three sections called movements.
A large group of musicians — often 50 to 100 people — playing instruments like violins, cellos, trumpets, and drums together. Also the name of a big piece written for that group.
A famous American brand of grand piano that many concert pianists prefer. Some Steinways in old churches are over 100 years old and still perfect for a concert.
How fast or slow a piece of music is being played. When a soloist is playing with an orchestra, the whole orchestra has to change tempo together with the soloist.

author: Riley Chen
